data.title: Samsung Electronics eyes investment in chip packaging plant in Vietnam
data.description:
SEOUL, April 9 (Reuters) - South Korean tech giant Samsung Electronics plans to invest in a chip packaging factory in Vietnam, a person familiar with the matter told Reuters, without elaborating on the value and timeline of the investment. 

A Samsung Electronics spokesperson was not immediately available for comment. 

* Vietnam's Ministry of Finance on Thursday said it wasworking with Samsung on a semiconductor project. * "The ministry is coordinating with other ministries andagencies to finalise and submit to the Prime Minister theMemorandum of Understanding between the Government of Vietnamand Samsung Group regarding the group's semiconductormanufacturing project in Vietnam," the ministry said in astatement. * A second source familiar with Samsung activities inVietnam said the company had signalled to Vietnamese authoritiesfor a long time its intention to launch semiconductor operationsin the country, without elaborating on the timing or size of thepossible investment. * Samsung is the largest single foreign investor in theSoutheast Asian country, having invested over $23 billion intothe country so far, according to the Vietnamese government.

(Reporting by Hyunjoo Jin; Additional reporting by Francesco Guarascio and Khanh Vu in Hanoi; Editing by Susan Fenton and Keith Weir)

Amazon.com Solicits Proxies from Shareholders
data.description: On April 9, 2026, Amazon.com, Inc. announced that it has filed a definitive proxy statement to solicit proxies from shareholders to vote against the shareholder proposal submitted by Brian Kariger, represented by As You Sow, and Mercy Investment Services, Inc, regarding a proposal on requesting additional reporting on impact of data centers on climate commitments, in connection with the annual meeting of shareholders scheduled to be held on May 20, 2026.

data.title: Brian Kariger Submits Shareholder Proposal to Amazon.com
data.description: On April 9, 2026, Brian Kariger, represented by As You Sow, and Mercy Investment Services, Inc, have submitted a shareholder proposal to Amazon.com, Inc., requesting the Company to issue a report explaining how it will meet the climate change-related commitments it has made on greenhouse gas emissions, given the massively growing energy demand from artificial intelligence and data centers that Amazon is planning to build.

data.title: China Tungsten And Hightech Materials Co.,Ltd Reports Earnings Results for the Full Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
China Tungsten And Hightech Materials Co.,Ltd reported earnings results for the full year ended December 31, 2025. For the full year, the company reported sales was CNY 17,638.51 million compared to CNY 14,779.49 million a year ago. Net income was CNY 1,280.67 million compared to CNY 991.25 million a year ago.

Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 0.58 compared to CNY 0.48 a year ago.

Novo Nordisk secures EU approval to streamline Wegovy delivery
data.description:
Danish pharmaceutical giant Novo Nordisk has received EMA approval allowing its obesity drug Wegovy to be transported for up to 48 hours at temperatures reaching 30 degrees Celsius. This development significantly reduces the reliance on cold-chain logistics.

The company anticipates that this change will lower distribution costs, streamline logistics, and facilitate home deliveries via pharmacies and e-commerce platforms. No financial details were disclosed.

"Home delivery of medicines is growing rapidly, driven by the convenience of having medications delivered directly to one's door – something we now expect in most other areas of our lives. Individuals with obesity using prescription medications can also face stigmatization, and we are therefore enthusiastic about the opportunity to simplify the distribution process while offering further discreet home delivery options," says Mike Doustdar, Executive Vice President at Novo Nordisk.

Oil gains 3% as fragile ceasefire, Hormuz keep supply risks elevated
data.description:
STORY: Oil prices rose on Thursday (April 9).

Markets were concerned about whether a two-week Middle East ceasefire will hold up.

And were unsure about energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

Brent crude futures were up around 3%, at roughly $98 a barrel.

While U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude also rose by a similar amount to just under $98.

Both benchmarks fell below $100 per barrel in the previous trading session.

That was on optimism the U.S.-Iranian ceasefire would result in the strait reopening.

But analysts still saw geopolitical risk, and there was no clarity on what U.S.-Iran talks would mean for oil flows.

The Strait of Hormuz connects supply from Gulf producers to global markets.

And it usually carries about a fifth of global oil and gas supply.

Shippers on Wednesday said they needed clarity on terms of the ceasefire before they continued transiting through the Strait of Hormuz.

Iranian media reported the country has issued maps to guide ships around mines and showing safe paths for passage.

Regional oil facilities also stayed under threat.

An oil industry source said Iran struck sites in nearby countries after the ceasefire.

It included a pipeline in Saudi Arabia which has been used to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.
